task.name: Stop Signal Task (STOP-IT)
reference_literature: Verbruggen, F., Aron, A. R., Band, G. P., Beste, C., Bissett, P. G., Brockett, A. T., ... & Boehler, C. N. (2019). A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task. eLife, 8, e46323.
Logan, G. D., & Cowan, W. B. (1984). On the ability to inhibit thought and action: A theory of an act of control. Psychological Review, 91(3), 295-327.
Verbruggen, F., & Logan, G. D. (2009). Models of response inhibition in the stop-signal and stop-change paradigms. Neuroscience & Biobehavioral Reviews, 33(5), 647-661.
Band, G. P., van der Molen, M. W., & Logan, G. D. (2003). Horse-race model simulations of the stop-signal procedure. Acta Psychologica, 112(2), 105-142.
Matzke, D., Verbruggen, F., & Logan, G. D. (2018). The stop-signal paradigm. Stevens' Handbook of Experimental Psychology and Cognitive Neuroscience, 5, 1-45.

stimuli.description: Stop signal trial — the go arrow has been replaced by a red stop image. The participant must withhold their response.

detection.selector: (function(){ var img = document.querySelector('#jspsych-image-keyboard-response-stimulus'); if(!img) return false; var s = img.src || ''; return s.indexOf('stop_left') !== -1 || s.indexOf('stop_right') !== -1; })()

stimuli.description: Go trial with left arrow — participant should press the left arrow key

detection.selector: (function(){ var img = document.querySelector('#jspsych-image-keyboard-response-stimulus'); if(!img) return false; var s = img.src || ''; return s.indexOf('go_left') !== -1 && s.indexOf('stop_') === -1; })()

stimuli.description: Go trial with right arrow — participant should press the right arrow key

detection.selector: (function(){ var img = document.querySelector('#jspsych-image-keyboard-response-stimulus'); if(!img) return false; var s = img.src || ''; return s.indexOf('go_right') !== -1 && s.indexOf('stop_') === -1; })()

task_specific.staircase_description: SSD increases by 50ms after successful stop, decreases by 50ms after failed stop, converging on ~50% inhibition rate

autocorrelation.rationale: Modest lag-1 autocorrelation is consistently observed in choice RT tasks (~0.1-0.2, Gilden 2001; Wagenmakers et al., 2004). In the stop-signal task, go-trial RTs show sequential dependencies as participants balance speed-accuracy tradeoffs.

fatigue_drift.rationale: The task has 5 blocks × ~48-64 trials = ~290+ total trials over ~25 minutes. A small fatigue drift of ~0.08ms/trial adds ~23ms over the full session, consistent with vigilance decrements in sustained attention tasks (Parasuraman & Davies, 1977).

post_error_slowing.rationale: Post-error slowing is a robust finding in speeded choice tasks (Rabbitt, 1966; Dutilh et al., 2012). In stop-signal tasks, post-error slowing of 20-80ms is typical after commission errors on go trials (Verbruggen & Logan, 2008).

condition_repetition.rationale: Stimulus repetition effects in two-choice tasks typically produce 5-15ms facilitation for repeated stimuli and a small cost for switches (Soetens et al., 1985). In the stop-signal task these effects are modest due to the binary stimulus set.

pink_noise.rationale: Long-range correlations (1/f noise) are ubiquitous in human RT series (Gilden, 2001; Wagenmakers et al., 2004). A Hurst exponent of ~0.75 and modest amplitude (15ms SD) captures the slow drift structure observed in extended RT tasks.

post_interrupt_slowing.rationale: After successful stop trials, participants typically slow their go responses on the next trial by 15-60ms (Verbruggen & Logan, 2008; Bissett & Logan, 2011). This post-stop slowing reflects a strategic adjustment in response caution.

between_subject_jitter.rationale: Individual differences in go RT mean are substantial in the stop-signal task (SD ~40-60ms across participants; Verbruggen et al., 2013). Accuracy and omission rates vary modestly. The left-right difference is small, so condition-level jitter is low. Sigma/tau variability reflects individual differences in the shape of RT distributions.

pilot.rationale: The practice block has 32 trials (NdesignReps_practice=4 × 8 trial basic design). With 25% stop trials, 16 trials should be sufficient to observe all 3 conditions (go_left, go_right, stop_signal). Running max 1 block (practice) validates all detection logic.

phase_detection.complete: (function(){ var el = document.querySelector('#jspsych-content'); if(!el) return false; var txt = el.innerText; return txt.indexOf('Thank you for your participation') !== -1; })()
phase_detection.feedback: (function(){ var el = document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus'); if(!el) return false; var txt = el.innerText || ''; return (txt.indexOf('correct') !== -1 || txt.indexOf('incorrect') !== -1 || txt.indexOf('too slow') !== -1 || txt.indexOf('too fast') !== -1 || txt.indexOf('remember') !== -1 || txt.indexOf('GO TRIALS') !== -1 || txt.indexOf('STOP-SIGNAL TRIALS') !== -1); })()

timing.response_window_js: (function(){ var img = document.querySelector('#jspsych-image-keyboard-response-stimulus'); if(!img) return false; var s = img.src || ''; return s.indexOf('/go_') !== -1 || s.indexOf('/stop_') !== -1; })()
timing.trial_context_js: (function(){ var img = document.querySelector('#jspsych-image-keyboard-response-stimulus'); if(!img) return ''; return img.src || ''; })()
